theory.question: You run one PostgreSQL instance that serves the checkout flow and also lets analysts run ad-hoc revenue reports. Explain the two access patterns colliding here.
theory.answer:
The same database is being asked to do two fundamentally different jobs: operational (OLTP) work and analytical (OLAP) work, which have opposite access patterns.

"An operational system typically looks up a small number of records by a key (this is called a point query)... Usually, an analytical query scans over a huge number of records and calculates aggregate statistics (such as count, sum, or average)."

The checkout flow is OLTP: many small, fixed, interactive queries that read and update individual records by key, representing the latest state of the data. The analyst reports are OLAP: few but complex ad-hoc queries that scan huge numbers of rows to compute aggregates, looking at history over time. The conflict is concrete: analytical scans are expensive and will hold locks, thrash the buffer cache, and consume CPU and I/O that the latency-sensitive checkout queries need. Analysts also want to write arbitrary SQL, which is a security and stability risk on the system that takes orders.

The typical fix is to keep a separate analytical system. "This separate database was called a data warehouse," holding a read-only copy of operational data, optimized for big scans. OLTP datasets are usually gigabytes to terabytes; analytical ones terabytes to petabytes.

Comment: Even when a vendor offers HTAP to run both in one system, internally it is usually an OLTP engine bolted to a separate analytical engine, so the distinction still drives the design.

theory.question: Design the flow that moves data out of a dozen operational databases into one place analysts can query freely, and name the steps.
theory.answer:
Extract the data from each operational system, transform it into an analysis-friendly schema, and load it into a single data warehouse; this is the ETL pipeline (or ELT when you load first and transform inside the warehouse).

"This process of getting data into the data warehouse is known as extract–transform–load (ETL)... Sometimes the order of the transform and load steps is swapped... resulting in ELT."

The motivation is that querying the operational systems directly is undesirable: data is spread across many systems (data silos), OLTP schemas are wrong for analytics, big analytical queries hurt OLTP performance, and the OLTP systems may sit on networks analysts cannot reach. A data warehouse solves this by holding a read-only copy from all the various OLTP systems, so "analysts can query to their hearts' content, without affecting OLTP operations."

Sources can also be external SaaS products reachable only through a vendor API (CRM, email marketing, payments); connector services like Fivetran, Singer, or Airbyte handle that extraction. A large enterprise typically has hundreds of operational databases (one per service) but a single warehouse, precisely so analysts can join data across systems in one query.

Comment: ELT became popular with cloud warehouses like Snowflake and BigQuery because cheap, scalable compute makes it practical to dump raw data first and transform it later with SQL.

theory.question: Your data scientists complain the relational data warehouse is too rigid for ML feature engineering on images and text. What storage approach fits, and how does it differ from a warehouse?
theory.answer:
Use a data lake: a centralized repository that holds copies of any potentially useful data as raw files, without imposing a single schema or format.

"The difference from a data warehouse is that a data lake simply contains files, without imposing any particular file format, data model, or schema."

A warehouse imposes a relational model queried via SQL, which works well for business analysts but poorly for data scientists who need feature engineering, NLP on text, or computer vision on images, and who prefer Pandas, R, and Spark over SQL. A data lake stores whatever you need: database records in Avro or Parquet, plus text, images, video, sensor readings, feature vectors, genome sequences. It is also cheaper because it can use commodity object storage like S3.

A key philosophy is the sushi principle, "raw data is better": the lake keeps data in the raw form produced by the operational systems, so each consumer transforms it into the shape that suits their needs rather than being forced into one warehouse schema up front. The lake often becomes an intermediate stop on the path to the warehouse.

Comment: Outputs can flow the other way too. Reverse ETL pushes results (e.g., an ML model's recommendations) from the analytical system back into operational systems so end users see them.

theory.question: Distinguish a system of record from a derived data system, and classify a primary Postgres table, a Redis cache, and an Elasticsearch index.
theory.answer:
A system of record holds the authoritative, canonical copy of data; a derived data system holds data computed from another source and can be rebuilt if lost.

"A system of record, also known as a source of truth, holds the authoritative or canonical version of data... If there is any discrepancy between another system and the system of record, the value in the system of record is (by definition) the correct one." Derived data "is the result of taking existing data from another system and transforming or processing it in some way. If you lose derived data, you can re-create it."

Classification: the primary Postgres table is the system of record (new facts are written here first, typically normalized, each fact represented once). The Redis cache and the Elasticsearch index are derived data: they duplicate information to speed up reads, and if they are wiped you can rebuild them from Postgres.

The crucial point is that this is not a property of the tool but of how you use it. A database is just a tool; the same engine can be a source of truth in one app and a derived store in another. Being explicit about which data is derived from which brings clarity to a confusing architecture, and it tells you which datasets need an update process when the source changes.

Comment: When the source changes you need a mechanism to update derived data; data pipelines and change-data-capture exist precisely to keep caches and indexes in sync with the system of record.

theory.question: Your team debates running databases on rented EC2 instances you manage yourself versus buying a managed cloud service. Lay out when each is actually cheaper.
theory.answer:
It depends mostly on your existing skills and how variable your load is: predictable load plus in-house operational expertise favors self-hosting, while unfamiliar systems and spiky load favor managed cloud services.

"If you already have experience setting up and operating the systems you need, and if your load is quite predictable... then it's often cheaper to buy your own machines and run the software on them yourself."

Managed services outsource operation to a provider that gains expertise across many customers, which can mean better service and frees your team for higher-level work, since hiring and training operators is expensive. They shine when load varies a lot: "cloud services have the advantage that they can make it easier to scale your computing resources up or down in response to changes in demand." Analytical systems are a prime example because a big query needs lots of parallel compute briefly, then sits idle.

The spectrum runs from bespoke in-house software, through self-hosted off-the-shelf software (download MySQL onto your server or an IaaS VM), to fully managed SaaS you only touch via an API. The build-vs-buy rule of thumb: do core competencies in-house, leave routine/commonplace work to a vendor.

Comment: The trade-off also includes control. Self-hosting lets you tune for your exact workload; a managed service rarely will customize for you, but you avoid the operational burden.

theory.question: Beyond price, what are the concrete downsides of building on a managed cloud service, and what mitigates the biggest one?
theory.answer:
The core downside is loss of control, with vendor lock-in being the most damaging form; using compatible/standard APIs is the main mitigation.

"If the service shuts down or becomes unacceptably expensive, or if the vendor changes their product in a way you don't like, you are at their mercy... so you'll be forced to migrate to an alternative service."

Specific risks: a missing feature can only be requested, not built; if the service goes down you can only wait; diagnosing performance bugs is hard because you cannot see OS metrics or server logs; a geopolitical conflict could lock you out via sanctions; and you must trust the provider with data security and regulatory compliance. The lock-in risk is sharpest because many cloud services expose no standard API, so switching is costly. "This risk is mitigated if alternative services expose a compatible API."

Despite these risks, building on cloud or hybrid approaches keeps growing. Cloud will not replace everything: legacy systems predate it, and specialist requirements remain in-house. Very latency-sensitive applications such as high-frequency trading need full control of the hardware, so they stay self-hosted.

Comment: This is why teams favor open standards and portable layers (e.g., Postgres-wire-compatible services, S3-compatible object stores) to keep an exit option and avoid being trapped.

theory.question: Explain what 'cloud native' architecture means and why separating storage from compute is central to it.
theory.answer:
Cloud native means an architecture designed from the ground up to build on higher-level cloud services rather than just renting VMs, and its defining move is disaggregating storage from compute.

"In a traditional systems architecture, the same computer is responsible for both storage (disk) and computation (CPU and RAM), but in cloud native systems, these two responsibilities have become somewhat separated, or disaggregated."

Merely running self-hosted software on IaaS VMs is not cloud native; you still administer generic CPU/RAM/disk yourself. Cloud native services instead layer on lower-level services: object stores like S3 store large files and hide the physical machines, automatically spreading data so you never run out of disk and lose nothing if a machine fails. Higher services build on those (Snowflake stores its data in S3).

Why separate storage and compute: cloud instances' local disks are treated as an ephemeral cache, not durable storage, because they vanish if the instance is replaced to adapt to load. Virtual disks (EBS) emulate a block device over the network, which adds overhead and makes every I/O a network call sensitive to glitches. So cloud native databases keep large data blocks in an object store and manage smaller values separately. Benefits: scale compute and storage independently, faster failure recovery, larger datasets.

Comment: This is also why such systems are multitenant: many customers share hardware for better utilization, which requires careful isolation so one tenant cannot hurt another's performance or security.

theory.question: How did the cloud and DevOps/SRE change the operations role, given that metered billing removes traditional capacity planning?
theory.answer:
Operations shifted from managing individual machines to managing services, automation, and cost; the need for operations did not shrink, it changed shape.

"Capacity planning becomes financial planning, and performance optimization becomes cost optimization."

Traditionally DBAs and sysadmins did machine-level work: capacity planning for disk space, provisioning machines, moving services, patching the OS. Many cloud services hide the machines behind an API (metered storage instead of fixed disks) and stay highly available despite failed machines. DevOps and Google's SRE practice merged development and operations, emphasizing automation over manual one-off jobs, ephemeral VMs over long-running servers, frequent updates, learning from incidents, and preserving institutional knowledge as people come and go.

A bifurcation occurred: infrastructure companies' ops teams specialize in running reliable services for many customers, while those customers spend as little effort as possible on infrastructure and instead focus on choosing services, integrating them, and migrating between them. Metered billing removes classic capacity planning but you must still track what you use so you don't waste money, and watch out for quotas. Security, inter-service interactions, load monitoring, and root-causing outages cannot be outsourced.

Comment: "good operations can often work around the limitations of bad software, but good software cannot run reliably with bad operations" — operability stays a first-class concern even in the cloud.

theory.question: List good reasons to make a system distributed across multiple machines, and the counterargument for staying on a single node.
theory.answer:
Distribute for inherent distribution, inter-service calls, fault tolerance, scalability, latency, elasticity, specialized hardware, legal data residency, and sustainability — but stay single-node when you can, because a single machine is far simpler and cheaper.

"For all these reasons, performing a task on a single machine is often much simpler and cheaper than setting up a distributed system."

A distributed system is several machines (nodes) communicating over a network. You go distributed when: users on separate devices must communicate; data lives in one service but is processed in another; you need redundancy to survive a machine/rack/datacenter failure; data or compute outgrows one machine; you want servers geographically near users to cut latency; you want elastic scale-up/down to pay only for what you use; different parts need different hardware (disk-heavy object stores vs GPU-heavy ML); data residency laws require keeping data in a jurisdiction; or you want to schedule jobs where renewable power is cheap.

The counterweight: every network call can fail or time out, leaving you unsure whether it was processed, so retries may be unsafe. Cross-service calls are far slower than in-process calls, troubleshooting needs observability/tracing, and keeping data consistent across services becomes the application's problem. CPUs, RAM, and disks have grown huge and reliable, and single-node databases like DuckDB and SQLite now handle many workloads.

Comment: "More nodes are not always faster"; a simple single-threaded program can beat a 100-core cluster, so do not rush into distribution.

theory.question: Argue for and against splitting a backend into microservices with a database per service versus keeping a monolith.
theory.answer:
Microservices let independent teams ship without coordinating, but they add real operational and data-consistency complexity, so they pay off mainly in large organizations.

"Microservices are primarily a technical solution to a people problem: allowing different teams to make progress independently without having to coordinate with each other."

In a microservices architecture each service has one well-defined purpose, exposes a network API, and is owned by one team. Advantages: services update independently, get their own hardware, and hide their implementation behind an API. Crucially, each service usually owns its database and does not share it, because "Sharing a database would effectively make the entire database structure a part of the service's API," making it hard to change and letting one service's queries hurt another's performance.

Costs: testing a service means running its dependencies; every service needs deploy, scaling, logging, monitoring, and alerting infrastructure (hence Kubernetes); APIs are hard to evolve without breaking clients (OpenAPI/gRPC help); and consistency across per-service databases becomes the application's job, since distributed transactions are rarely used here. In a small company with few teams, microservices are usually unnecessary overhead, and the simplest implementation is preferable.

Comment: Serverless/FaaS goes further by outsourcing infrastructure entirely — you pay per execution and the provider allocates resources on demand — at the cost of execution time limits, restricted runtimes, and cold-start latency.

theory.question: Contrast cloud computing with high-performance computing (supercomputing) on fault handling and networking.
theory.answer:
Supercomputers run large batch scientific jobs and handle a node failure by checkpointing and restarting the whole cluster, whereas cloud systems must keep serving users continuously and so tolerate faults at the software level.

"If a node fails, a common solution is to simply stop the entire cluster workload, repair the faulty node, and then restart the computation from the last checkpoint."

HPC targets compute-intensive tasks: weather forecasting, climate and molecular dynamics simulation, PDEs. Cloud targets online services and business data systems needing high availability, so stopping the whole cluster to recover is unacceptable. Networking differs too: supercomputer nodes communicate via shared memory and RDMA with high trust among users, often over specialized topologies (meshes, toruses) tuned to known communication patterns. Cloud datacenters use IP/Ethernet in Clos topologies for high bisection bandwidth, assume mutually untrusting tenants, and therefore need isolation (VMs), encryption, and authentication. Cloud nodes can also span multiple geographic regions, while supercomputers assume nodes are physically close.

Large-scale analytical systems sometimes borrow HPC techniques, which is why the comparison matters, but this book focuses on continually available services.

Comment: The fault-handling philosophy is the key takeaway — cloud's "keep serving while nodes fail" assumption is what drives replication, redundancy, and the rest of distributed-systems design covered later.

theory.question: You store users' location history and an append-only event log. How do GDPR's right-to-be-forgotten and data minimization reshape this design?
theory.answer:
Privacy regulation makes legal requirements a first-class architectural constraint: you must be able to actually delete personal data on request, even from supposedly immutable structures, and you should avoid collecting data you don't need.

"The GDPR grants individuals the right to have their data erased on request (sometimes known as the right to be forgotten). However... many data systems rely on immutable constructs such as append-only logs."

The tension is concrete: append-only logs and derived datasets (caches, indexes, ML training data) duplicate information, so deleting one record in the middle of an immutable file, and from everything derived from it, becomes a real engineering challenge with no standard, regulator-blessed recipe. GDPR deliberately states high-level principles rather than mandating technologies, leaving interpretation to engineers.

Data minimization (Datensparsamkeit) pushes back against the "store everything speculatively" big-data mindset: GDPR says personal data may be collected only for a specified explicit purpose, not reused for others, and not kept longer than necessary. For location history specifically, the cost calculus is not just the S3 bill: it includes breach liability, reputational damage, legal fines, and the safety risk that location data could reveal criminalized behavior or be compelled by authorities. Sometimes the right answer is not to store the data at all.

Comment: Practically, teams design log compaction, crypto-shredding (delete the encryption key to render data unrecoverable), and tombstone-driven deletion that propagates to derived stores to reconcile immutability with erasure rights.

practice.task: [PostgreSQL] Separate an overloaded OLTP database from analytics by designing an ETL target schema for a star-style sales report.
practice.answer:
Adopt the star-schema warehouse above, but make the pipeline operationally robust and choose storage that matches the access pattern. Migration steps: (1) backfill the warehouse from existing OLTP history; (2) switch ongoing loads to change-data-capture (e.g., logical replication / Debezium) so new orders stream into the warehouse continuously instead of a nightly bulk dump, narrowing freshness from hours to seconds; (3) make loads idempotent and keyed so reruns and retries do not double-count revenue; (4) cut analysts over to the warehouse and revoke their access to the OLTP system of record.

For storage, prefer a column-oriented analytical store (ClickHouse or DuckDB over Parquet files in object storage) rather than row-oriented Postgres, because category/country aggregations scan few columns over many rows; columnar storage and compression make these scans dramatically cheaper, which is exactly the OLTP-vs-OLAP layout difference. This also enables ELT: land raw data cheaply, transform with SQL in the warehouse.

Operational concerns: monitor ETL/CDC lag, alert on failed or stalled loads, validate row counts and revenue totals against the source, and handle schema evolution in the OLTP tables. Edge cases: late-arriving or corrected orders (use upserts and slowly-changing dimensions), deleted customers (propagate deletions for GDPR compliance, since the warehouse is derived data that must honor erasure), and time-zone handling in dim_date. Trade-off: CDC and a separate columnar system add infrastructure and cost, justified once analytical load and dataset size outgrow what a single Postgres instance can serve without hurting checkout.
